findings.description: Bare `except: pass` in transform_batch silently swallows all exceptions including KeyboardInterrupt, SystemExit, and unexpected runtime errors. Failed records are dropped with no logging, no counter increment, and no signal to the caller.
findings.evidence: except Exception: pass — no log, no _error_count increment, no re-raise

findings.description: normalize_currency returns the unconverted record unchanged when no exchange rate is found, after only logging a warning. The amount remains in a non-USD currency but currency field stays as-is, so downstream aggregation silently mixes currencies as if they were USD.
findings.evidence: return record  # returns unconverted record silently — currency and amount are inconsistent with warehouse expectations

findings.description: _processed_count and _error_count are module-level globals incremented without a lock. Multiple threads call `_processed_count += 1` concurrently, producing a race condition that yields an incorrect (usually under-counted) metric.
findings.evidence: global _processed_count incremented in worker threads via transform_batch without threading.Lock

findings.description: _error_count is declared and reset but never incremented anywhere in the codebase. The logged summary reports `_processed_count` and `written` but omits error count entirely, so errors are invisible to operators.
findings.evidence: _error_count = 0 set in run_pipeline but never incremented; logger.info omits it

findings.description: all_results.extend(transformed) is called from multiple threads without a lock. list.extend is not thread-safe across CPython releases in all cases, and even if GIL protects the list mutation, interleaved results are non-deterministic.
findings.evidence: all_results defined in run_pipeline closure, extended from multiple threads concurrently

findings.description: validate_transaction is defined but never called in the pipeline (transform_batch, run_pipeline). Invalid records—missing fields, negative amounts—pass through transformation and reach the database write step.
findings.evidence: validate_transaction defined but no call site in transform_batch or run_pipeline

findings.description: When num_workers does not evenly divide len(raw_records), batch_size = len(raw_records) // num_workers. If len(raw_records) < num_workers, batch_size is 0 and the last worker gets all records while some workers get empty slices—or a ZeroDivisionError if raw_records is empty.
findings.evidence: batch_size = len(raw_records) // num_workers with no guard for empty input or batch_size == 0

findings.description: normalize_currency mutates the input dict in-place (sets original_amount, original_currency, amount, currency keys on record). This means the caller's original record is modified as a side-effect, making retries and debugging harder.
findings.evidence: record["original_amount"] = ...; record["amount"] = ... — modifies the passed-in dict directly

findings.description: write_results catches all exceptions per record but does not commit or rollback; the docstring defers commit to the caller. run_pipeline never commits or rolls back either, leaving the transaction open on success or partial failure.
findings.evidence: # Don't commit here — let the caller decide; run_pipeline does not call db_connection.commit()

findings.description: write_results inserts each record individually in a loop with no batching. At scale this is O(n) round-trips, likely to time out or saturate the database under large inputs.
findings.evidence: for record in results: db_connection.execute(...) — one INSERT per record

findings.description: The pipeline has no tests (no test file referenced, no assertions, no CFT coverage). The validation function has no test proving it rejects bad records; silent failure paths in transform_batch have no coverage.
findings.evidence: No test file; no assertions in code; critical silent-failure branches unreachable by any visible test

findings.description: exchange rates dict is passed in from outside with no validation of staleness, completeness, or source. A stale or empty rates dict silently produces incorrect USD amounts across the entire batch.
findings.evidence: rates: Dict[str, float] parameter—no timestamp, no version, no completeness check

findings.description: data_source.fetch_all() loads the entire dataset into memory at once. For large data lakes this will cause OOM or extreme memory pressure; no streaming or pagination is used.
findings.evidence: raw_records = data_source.fetch_all() — full dataset loaded before any processing

findings.description: The SQL INSERT uses %(key)s parameter substitution from a raw user-originated dict without field allowlisting. Extra keys in the record are silently ignored by psycopg2 but the dict keys come from external data, creating a surface for unexpected behavior if the DB driver changes.
findings.evidence: VALUES (%(transaction_id)s, ...) passing full record dict—extra attacker-controlled keys present

clean.location: lines 114-120 (thread creation and join loop structure)
